data.title: Tsinghua, Microsoft researchers train AI model using synthetic data, Nvidia chips
data.description:
Tsinghua University and Microsoft researchers have developed a synthetic data pipeline for training artificial intelligence models without the need for real-world data, using chips from leading US chip designer Nvidia.
The pipeline called SynthSmith was able to develop a small coding model that outperformed a model twice its size, potentially addressing a key bottleneck of real-world data scarcity for improving AI models, according to the paper published on open access repository arXiv on January 11.
“In-depth analysis reveals that scaling laws hold on our synthetic dataset,” said the researchers from Tsinghua University, Microsoft Research Asia and Wuhan University.
Synthetic data mimicking real-world data is generated by AI algorithms. As new real-world data becomes scarce, AI researchers are experimenting with synthetic data to continue improving AI models.

Using SynthSmith, the researchers trained an X-Coder model with 7 billion parameters that scored higher than models with 14 billion parameters on major coding benchmarks despite using less data and none from the real world, the paper said.
The computational power required for the experiment was provided by 128 Nvidia H20 chips for 220 hours during the supervised fine-tuning stage and 32 H200 chips for seven days during the reinforcement learning stage, the paper said.
As the AI industry has shifted towards agentic reasoning models that “think” before generating outputs and which can perform tasks over long periods, this has placed the focus on scaling up the amount of computational resources needed during model inference, or deployment, rather than just during the pre-training stage.
Wu Jie, the paper’s lead author and a master’s student at Tsinghua University, said that the main reason their new innovation had yet to be scaled up to the industry’s largest models, such as those with over 100 billion or trillions of parameters, was computational constraints, “rather than limitations of the pipeline itself”.
By open-sourcing their research, the team wants others in the research community to extend the research without having to bear the training costs.
The Trump administration has made both the H20 and H200 chips available to Chinese companies after extensive lobbying by Nvidia to lift export controls. The H20 is optimised for model inference, while the H200 is the most powerful AI processor available to Chinese AI companies for training cutting-edge models.

data.title: AXA becomes second insurer to redomicile from Bermuda to Hong Kong
data.description:
French insurer AXA’s Hong Kong unit, one of the city’s largest life and general insurers, has become the second insurance company to move its domicile to Hong Kong from Bermuda, according to a statement on Monday.
At least three other local insurers, including the local unit of Canada’s Sun Life, were planning the same move after a law change in May simplified the process, according to industry players.
AXA was the first to begin the process after the rule change, but Manulife International was the first insurer to complete the move, in December.
“The redomiciliation underscores our strong confidence in Hong Kong’s strategic importance and growth potential, and reflects our unwavering commitment to serving customers with resilience and innovation,” said Sally Wan Yuen-wai, CEO of AXA Greater China.
“With this redomiciliation now complete, we are even better positioned to capitalise on Hong Kong’s vibrant momentum as Asia’s leading risk-management and insurance hub.”
Wan said Hong Kong was a gateway to the Greater Bay Area, where insurance penetration was low, making it a high-growth area. The Greater Bay Area comprises Hong Kong, Macau and nine mainland cities in Guangdong province.
“There’s clearly a high growing need for good quality healthcare and wealth planning solutions,” Wan said. “Opportunities in wealth protection and legacy planning for high-net-worth clients are also promising.”
From Monday, AXA China Region Insurance Company (Bermuda) will be renamed AXA China Region Insurance Company (Hong Kong) to reflect the move.

The Insurance Authority said AXA’s re-domiciliation reinforced “Hong Kong’s strong appeal as an international financial centre and the industry’s commitment to contributing to Hong Kong as a regional insurance hub”.
“The Insurance Authority will continue to work closely with all relevant parties to enable interested insurers to re-domicile to Hong Kong under the regime, supporting the Government’s vision to strengthen headquarters economy,” it said.
About a dozen insurers in Hong Kong are incorporated in Bermuda. They chose the Caribbean nation for tax purposes because of a Hong Kong government estate duty, which was eliminated in 2006. Insurance companies that wanted to redomicile after the duty was eliminated could not easily do so due to a lack of legal support.
The law change in May allows companies incorporated overseas to establish themselves in the city while maintaining their legal identities and business continuity. Previously, a company that wanted to move its domicile had to close an existing entity and shift all assets and transactions to a new firm in Hong Kong.
Secretary for Financial Services and the Treasury Christopher Hui Ching-yu said AXA’s relocation showed that the new law helped to attract companies to the city. The Companies Registry had approved 14 cases and was processing 30 more applications from financial firms, holding companies, insurers and fund houses, he added.
“The government will continue to work with the companies to facilitate them to move to domicile in Hong Kong,” Hui said on the sidelines of the Asian Financial Forum on Monday.
In October, a unit of PetroChina Investment (Hong Kong) became the first company approved to reincorporate in the city under the new law.
In September, two Hong Kong-listed companies under China Resources Group announced plans to redomicile from the Cayman Islands: China Resources Beverage (Holdings), the mainland’s second-largest water bottler under the brand C’estbon, and its sister company, China Resources Building Materials Technology Holdings.

data.title: Legislators, judiciary must ‘support’ Hong Kong’s executive-led government: Xia
data.description:
Beijing’s point man on Hong Kong affairs has called on the city to improve its executive-led governance model, arguing that the legislative and judicial branches should support the government as they perform “on the same stage” rather than undermine one another.
Xia Baolong, director of the Hong Kong and Macau Affairs Office, on Monday warned against any attempts by foreign and disruptive forces to promote the idea of separation of powers, arguing such views undermined the authority of the local and central governments.
In his first major address to the city’s new cohort of legislators at a closed-door seminar organised by the semi-official Chinese Association of Hong Kong and Macau Studies think tank, Xia reminded lawmakers to uphold high standards of personal conduct and serve as role models for residents.
He also urged the city to improve its executive-led system and called on the legislative and judicial branches to support that model of governance.
“The crux of insisting and improving the executive-led model is for the chief executive and his administration to strengthen their sense of ownership over Hong Kong and Macau and bear primary responsibility for governance,” he said.

“Although the executive, legislative and judicial branches have their respective divisions of labour, their ultimate goal is the same,” he added. “They perform on the same stage – they should support one another rather than pull the rug out from under each other. The legislative and judicial branches should defend the executive-led model and respect that principle.”
Xia said the executive-led system had shown its resilience by restoring stability in Hong Kong, with the city learning lessons after the model was challenged in recent years – an apparent reference to the social unrest of 2019.
“Anti-China and external forces are [still] trying to undermine the executive-led system under the Basic Law and advocate the notion of separation of powers to weaken the authority of the chief executive and the local administration. They do this to damage and resist the authority of the central government,” he said.
“That is absolutely unacceptable and we must be highly vigilant against their evolving tactics to oppose, damage and disrupt the executive-led government.”
Other officials, politicians and lawmakers tuned in to a live stream of the seminar on Monday at the Hong Kong government headquarters in Admiralty.

data.title: Ex-PLA soldier jailed for 8 months over illegal ride-hailing trips in Hong Kong
data.description:
A former People’s Liberation Army soldier has been sentenced to eight months in prison for illegally carrying passengers in his Tesla Model S luxury sedan using a ride-hailing platform while in Hong Kong as a tourist.
Fanling Court on Monday heard that Zeng Yulin, 39, travelled to Hong Kong from Shenzhen between June and August last year to offer paid rides using online mapping service Amap, earning more than HK$73,000 (US$9,400) in two months.
He told Acting Principal Magistrate Veronica Heung Shuk-han that he took the risk because he needed to support his family and faced financial difficulties.
However, the magistrate noted that the father of two had previously spent more than HK$450,000 on cars, including the one he used to commit the offences, showing that his financial struggles were “self-induced”.
“The defendant deliberately came to Hong Kong to commit a crime with a plan,” she said.
“He was struggling with money because of his own doing. It is different from ordinary people who feel the pinch [of financial hardship].”
The defendant pleaded guilty to 17 counts of breaching conditions of stay and one count each of driving a motor vehicle for the carriage of passengers for hire or reward and using a vehicle without third-party insurance.
Heung said the defendant had travelled between Shenzhen and Hong Kong on 17 occasions between June 20 and August 21. He provided 691 trips by using someone else’s name to open service accounts.
Driving on the road without third-party insurance posed a danger to pedestrians and other drivers in the event of an accident, the magistrate added.

Police investigations revealed that the defendant had borrowed the Hong Kong identity card and driving licence of a former schoolmate in Huizhou to create two ride-hailing accounts with service providers Go Full and Universe Call, both linked to Amap.
Amap is operated by Alibaba Group Holding, the parent company of the South China Morning Post.
It is currently unlawful for private vehicle drivers to accept paying passengers without a hire-car permit in Hong Kong, with platforms including Uber, Tada, Amap and Didi Chuxing operating without regulation in the city.
Defence counsel earlier told the court that Zeng was a former squad leader in the PLA’s ground forces and had last worked as a shopping centre manager in mainland China before becoming unemployed in 2024.
Heung read mitigation letters for Zeng, including some from his former army colleagues, noting that he had been praised as a responsible man.
In sentencing, she granted a one-third reduction to reflect his timely guilty plea.

data.title: Open Questions|Jeffrey Ding on why diffusion, not innovation, is the secret to AI success
data.description:
Jeffrey Ding is an assistant professor of political science at George Washington University. He is the author of Technology and the Rise of Great Powers, an award-winning book exploring the impact of technology on geopolitical competition, as well as the founder of the ChinAI newsletter, which tracks developments in China’s artificial intelligence (AI) industry.
In this interview, Ding explains why “diffusion”, not innovation, will determine whether China or the US will prevail in the AI race, the Trump administration’s “counterproductive” policies around the technology, misconceptions about the two countries’ respective strengths in the field and why human capital is the key to victory.
This interview first appeared in SCMP Plus. For other interviews in the Open Questions series, click here.
You recently wrote an article for Rand Corporation, the influential US think tank, in which you argued that the US is “training for the wrong race” in AI. What did you mean?
The main reason I wrote that piece was to clarify what I see as a lot of confusion out there about what the US and China are actually competing for in AI. Others have already articulated different visions of this US-China AI race, but I wanted to put forth clearly that there is one type of race that I think the US should optimise for, which is this “diffusion marathon” rather than a sprint towards a clear finish line.
The “diffusion marathon” refers to the progress the two countries make in spreading AI throughout their respective economies.
This can be contrasted with a vision of the AI race as an “innovation sprint” – the view of many in US national security circles – where the key question is which country can innovate its way to developing an artificial general intelligence (AGI) with “God-like powers”, in the words of Jake Sullivan, the National Security Adviser under the Joe Biden administration.
In their view, this sprint will be decided in the next few years, and will give the winning country a permanent technological lead and decisive military advantage, whereas the “diffusion marathon” that I am putting forward happens over decades, with no obvious finish line.
Your book argued that diffusion matters more than technological innovation for determining overall national power. Why is that?
It’s because AI is a general-purpose technology (GPT), something that can spread throughout an economy, rather than just be limited to certain industries or sectors.
If you look at the examples of previous GPTs such as electricity, they stand out as historical engines of growth because they boosted productivity and transformed entire economies. And history tells us that a country’s ability to sustain economic growth in the long run is central to its geopolitical and military influence.
The difficulty for policymakers is that the potential economic gains from a GPT will arrive only after a long process of diffusion.
For example, while the Soviet Union was an innovation leader in electricity, it was the US that became the biggest diffuser of the technology by 1970. Even then, the boost to US productivity from electrification was only seen four decades after the first electric dynamo emerged, because complementary innovations such as steam turbines had to be made for these gains to be realised.
When many journalists and policymakers think about scientific and technological power, they often focus on a set of indicators revolving around innovation capacity: a country’s ability to pioneer new-to-the-world inventions. For example, how much is a country spending on cutting-edge research and development? What is the average international ranking of your top three research universities?
Specifically for AI, the indicators people tend to focus on are top AI start-ups or which AI labs or institutions have published the most highly cited papers that year.
However, there is another set of indicators to measure a country’s scientific and technological prowess, what I call diffusion capacity. There, it’s not necessarily about the frontier AI companies or universities, your Stanfords or Tsinghuas, or your OpenAIs and DeepSeeks.
Rather, it’s about which country can take the technological breakthroughs incubated in these frontier institutions and transfer them to small and medium-sized businesses in an inland province like Qinghai, or a Midwestern state like Iowa where I grew up in the US.
This is the most important part of US-China AI competition, based on years of historically grounded research into past industrial revolutions and GPTs.
Overall, the Trump administration has done much more damage to the US’ ability to diffuse AI over the next few decades than it has helped

What is your assessment of the Trump administration’s AI policies? It appears to have placed more emphasis on AI diffusion than the previous Biden administration, which seemed more focused on sprinting towards AGI.
It’s been interesting to see how so many people are now paying attention to AI diffusion, which was a very neglected topic back when I started writing my book.
The Trump administration’s AI action plan does gesture at some of these components of the diffusion marathon. For example, it says that the slow adoption of AI is the key bottleneck holding back the technology, not necessarily a lack of cutting-edge AI models or applications.
But my view is that this is akin to treating an ankle sprain while the patient is bleeding out. Overall, the Trump administration has done much more damage to the US’ ability to diffuse AI over the next few decades than it has helped.
A very counterproductive action has been the administration’s war against the broader higher education ecosystem. The lasting damage, for me, is not necessarily any specific dispute with top institutions like Harvard or Cornell, but rather the administration’s disinvestment from education initiatives that help social mobility, support community colleges and the base of higher education institutions more broadly.
Another counterproductive action has been the intensified restrictions on international students, which significantly weakens the US’ ability to tap into a broad base of AI engineering talent drawn from all over the world.
Both these actions are counterproductive because they damage what I call the US’ “GPT skill infrastructure” – the education system that trains the engineers needed to help your country diffuse a GPT.
If you look at why the US was better able to diffuse electrical technologies than the Soviet Union, it was because the US had a broader base of engineers that gave it an edge in transferring knowledge between academia and industry.
There’s low-hanging fruit that has been left unpicked, such as the Chips and Science Act signed in 2022. The chips plank of the act, domestic production of semiconductors, has been swiftly implemented. But the science plank – workforce development initiatives focusing on science, technology, engineering and mathematics (STEM) fields – has been much slower in implementation, with several programmes yet to receive full funding from Congress.
Many leading AI experts believe that a powerful AGI could be developed within the next decade. Does it not make sense for the US to run both the innovation sprint and diffusion marathon at the same time?
The key thing to note for US policymakers is that they can’t have it all. If they are going to prioritise the diffusion marathon over other possible versions of the AI race, then there are going to be trade-offs. The AI action plan has many headline-grabbing announcements about AI adoption, but they don’t make a meaningful difference if you’re doing so much damage to some of the foundations of the US’ AI diffusion capacity.
One of the justifications for stricter restrictions on Chinese international students and researchers is the fear that cutting-edge AI innovations could be leaked to China. But previous industrial revolutions clearly show that it is impossible for any one country to monopolise foundational innovations in GPTs, which means that any real benefits from these policies are limited.
This is a clear example of a policy that is more optimised for a vision of the AI race as a short two or three-year sprint to AGI, because you don’t want your adversary getting there first through espionage. But the policy is counterproductive from an AI diffusion standpoint. So you have to make a choice about what race you are running and weigh the trade-offs accordingly.
An important caveat is that my arguments rest on the assumption that AI will diffuse in a similar fashion as past industrial revolutions and GPTs like electricity. I’m totally open to the idea that AI recursively self-improving could one day lead to some sort of fast take-off in capabilities that thwarts my projections.
But because most of the discourse in the US has been dominated by this vision of AI as a special, once-in-a-lifetime technology, what I’m trying to do is bring some balance to the discussion and remind everyone of these lessons of history.
I’ve been studying these topics since 2017 and have always been surrounded by people who say that AGI is two years away. It’s always two years away from being two years away. For me, it’s a reason to update more towards this GPT diffusion view, but ultimately technology forecasting is very difficult.


How effective have US export controls on advanced semiconductor chip sales to China been? The Trump administration has justified its recent loosening of such restrictions by arguing that they hamper the US’ ability to diffuse its AI globally.
Export controls are very much rooted in this “fortress America” approach to the AI race, one focused on preventing cutting-edge technology from leaking to China because you’re focused on which country is going to create the greatest breakthrough in AI in the next couple of years.
Export controls are not oriented towards this AI diffusion marathon. By the time AI has made a meaningful difference to the trajectory of national economies, export controls will have already eroded Nvidia’s monopoly on the most cutting-edge chips. So basically what you’re doing with any restrictions now is hurting a US company for not much benefit in the long run, while also damaging China and the US’ ability to cooperate on other issues such as AI safety and governance.
The US should focus on running a better race rather than obsessing over the impossible task of stopping others from running at all. The lifting of chip controls will not necessarily be a net positive for US diffusion strategy, but I also do not think it will make a meaningful difference in China’s ability to win an AI diffusion marathon.
As one of the world’s leading experts on China’s AI ecosystem, what is your assessment of China’s AI diffusion capacity?
First off, it’s hard to accurately measure diffusion capacity, because GPTs historically take a very long time to diffuse. However, I still believe China faces a “diffusion deficit”. Put simply, it means China’s innovation capacity outpaces its diffusion capacity, contrary to the popular framing these days of China being better at diffusing than innovating in AI.
Historically, countries that face this diffusion deficit can struggle to sustain their rise because what actually matters at the end of the day is productivity growth, which is the key ingredient to unlocking long-term economic growth.
If you look at what people usually cite as evidence of China’s AI strengths, they are often innovation indicators rather than diffusion indicators: how many papers are being submitted to top AI conferences, the strength of its frontier models and so forth.
One of the most cited statistics is that 47 per cent of the world’s top AI researchers are from China. But that figure came from a random sample of a single year’s attendance at the NeurIPS conference, widely considered the top AI academic conference.
In the same study, they found that 42 per cent of these top researchers worked in the US, while 28 per cent worked in China. Having many top AI researchers be of Chinese ethnicity or background does not mean your country has a broad talent base.
One diffusion indicator I track instead is how many universities in the two countries have at least one affiliated researcher who has published at an established AI conference.
Those numbers have not changed that much over the last few years, with China at around 30 to 40 in any given year, while the US has around 150 to 160. This is a better indicator to track, because you need a broad and deep bench of universities to train “good enough” AI engineers to help you diffuse AI across the country.
Once you lower the quality baseline beyond the top 1 per cent of researchers to focus on the broader pool of talent that each country has to help diffuse AI, you find that the US still has an advantage.




Chinese authorities have moved aggressively to make AI a central plank of the education system, including AI instruction for primary school students and a host of new majors for embodied intelligence and robotics. How important are these for increasing China’s diffusion capacity?
What’s important to note about a GPT like AI is that you don’t know how it will evolve. So when there’s too much top-down involvement where the central government optimises for a particular trajectory of AI, policies that make sense now could be outdated six months later if the direction of the technology changes.
That’s where I think China’s model could suffer. China’s AI education policy has been very centralised, where the authorities have strongly encouraged universities to develop AI-related programmes and offer AI majors. But it’s hard to know where the technology is going. If we were having this conversation even just three years ago, maybe we wouldn’t even be talking about large language models (LLMs) but something else entirely. So a more decentralised approach might be more appropriate.
It’s true that China has many AI engineers, but it’s not just the number that matters. It’s also about whether there are good linkages between academia, industry and other parts of the science and technological ecosystem.
The US leads the world in AI publications with at least one co-author from industry and at least one co-author from academia. China has barriers to academia-industry collaboration, such as a lack of resources for small and medium-sized businesses to partner with universities, as well as less robust linkages with universities outside the top centres of excellence.
To improve its diffusion capacity, China needs to invest more in human capital. But so far, the evidence points to the Chinese government continuing to prioritise investments in research and development.
China has been very strong in consistently meeting cutting-edge R&D targets in targeted sectors like robotics or new energy vehicles, but this has not extended to the meeting of education funding targets.
For example, China’s public expenditures on education as a proportion of gross domestic product are lower than those of other newly industrialised countries like Brazil and South Africa, while its R&D spending as a percentage of GDP far exceeds those countries.
The takeaway should be that it’s very difficult for either the US or China to implement a diffusion-centred AI strategy. By definition, the policies I’m calling for – investing in community colleges to provide alternative pathways to cultivating AI talent, broadening the base of higher education institutions to train AI engineers – accrue to dispersed interests.
No one industry is going to lobby for these types of policies, whereas it’s much easier for both China and the US to target specific sectors like chips or robotics.
It’s unlikely AI will make a significant difference to economic productivity before 2030, which is why there’s all this talk about a potential ‘bubble’

What is your assessment of how China has run the AI race in the last year? In particular, the emergence of DeepSeek made many people more confident about the country’s AI trajectory.
One of the things I’ve been tracking in the past year is how exactly businesses and local governments in China have been adopting AI.
After the DeepSeek moment in January, you saw models being integrated in state-owned enterprises, hospitals and local governments across China. When I dug deeper into this, what surprised me was seeing all these reports about a DeepSeek “all-in-one” machine – a separate piece of hardware that these end users were using.
While I’m open to the idea that AI could diffuse through other channels, AI is still fundamentally a software-based breakthrough. Eventually, of course, it will diffuse into embodied hardware, but at this stage, the most efficient way to adopt AI is how you would adopt any other piece of software, which is through a software-as-a-service (SaaS) subscription model where you embed and connect it to all your other workflows rather than having a separate piece of hardware.
It remains to be seen just how much substance there is to the AI Plus initiative announced in August, which was billed as an AI diffusion document. It did not come with guaranteed funding numbers, but new initiatives such as rewarding faculty and students with credits for open-source software contributions are indeed insightful and innovative.
One question I have is about timelines. According to the document, the Chinese government expects a 90 per cent AI adoption rate across the entire economy by 2030, five years from now. Any reasoned and informed assessment of AI diffusion timelines would see that as a complete non-starter because GPTs take decades to diffuse across an economy, not years.
It’s unlikely AI will make a significant difference to economic productivity before 2030, which is why there’s all this talk about a potential AI “bubble” in the US because it will probably take a prolonged period of gestation for AI to make a meaningful impact on the economy.
While I am arguing that the US is running the wrong AI race, I’m not saying China is running a perfect one either.
A big difference from a year ago is China’s push for open-source AI, making the foundational model layer a sort of infrastructure for AI applications to be built upon. What is open-source’s relevance from a diffusion standpoint?
Open-source technology is something I wish I had looked into more when I delved into historical cases of GPT diffusion. There are all these other factors that are going to influence the pace of diffusion beyond just human capital, which is what I focus on.
Open-source software is a relatively new phenomenon that didn’t exist in past industrial revolutions. The first thing to note is that open source is the tool of the challenger – it’s a way to catch up because it means you’re not competing as individual companies but rather as an entire ecosystem of all these different collaborators.
From a diffusion perspective, I think a very vibrant open-source ecosystem is more conducive to diffusion. But just because DeepSeek has better open-source models than, say, Meta Platforms’ Llama models doesn’t mean China is going to be ahead in terms of adopting open-source AI.
In fact, many of the earliest and most stable implementations of DeepSeek came from US companies like Perplexity and Together AI. The US also has a very vibrant open-source ecosystem, even if you’re not creating the most leading-edge open-source models.
Most of the foundational open-source frameworks for AI were built by US firms, like Google’s TensorFlow and Meta’s PyTorch. In open source, you can’t just look at the LLM layer – you also must look at the deeper foundational layers.
There are now some US players such as OpenAI and Ai2 building their own open-source models. But these initiatives are not necessarily optimising for what I care about: economy-wide diffusion of AI for broad productivity gains. Both China and the US are looking to become self-reliant, but I don’t care where the open-source software comes from as long as you can build on top of the software and integrate it into productive workflows.
Who cares where electricity comes from? What matters is that you can adopt it and get productivity gains from it.


AI is a broad term. While the US prioritises AI software, China is deploying robotics in manufacturing and introducing delivery robots and drones at a rapid rate. Are these not more conducive to productivity gains than AI chatbots?
That’s an interesting debate to be had, but ultimately AI diffusion cannot be confined to any one industry. Maybe embodied AI and robotics will help with different manufacturing sectors, but your local government or state-owned enterprise does not necessarily need an embodied robot to help them become more efficient.
You also cannot just equate robotics with AI, because it’s too early to tell how much deep learning is really happening in these robotic applications. The deep learning paradigm is ultimately what will lead to huge productivity gains.
My prediction is that SaaS is going to be the dominant diffusion pathway for AI – but Chinese businesses are not that willing to pay for such services. I say this not as some dogmatic view of how AI will eventually diffuse, as it’s possible that the most impactful advances from AI will not even come from LLMs.
My point is that when people say China is racing ahead on AI diffusion, they often use examples that are not sustainable and efficient integrations of AI that will drive productivity growth.
This time last year, you expressed confidence that the US will win the AI race in the long term. Are you still confident?
What I wrote one year ago still holds true. When you consider the AI competition as this diffusion marathon, the US is still very well positioned to win that race.
Part of the reason there’s so much swing in the discourse about US-China AI competition, from the consensus that the US is clearly in front to now more talk of China being ahead, is that AI is a GPT and therefore it will continuously change and evolve.
There’s so much confusion because people are often talking about different types of AI competitions. Some people are talking about who is building the latest and greatest LLM, some are talking about specific application sectors like robotics or facial recognition, and some are focusing on military AI applications.
For me, the diffusion marathon is the one that matters the most. In that competition, it’s still very early days, but I have a lot of confidence that the US is in the best position to win that race because of its broader pool of education and training institutions that can cultivate AI engineers who will spread this GPT throughout the entire economy.
However, the Trump administration has taken some counterproductive actions, and it’s much easier to lose this race than to know exactly how to win it.
What are some open questions you are exploring?
An open question I’m tracking is how best to measure AI diffusion. One interesting way is looking at how many companies in different countries have reached particular thresholds of computing token usage for LLMs. Being able to measure that for small and medium-sized enterprises could be a good way of tracking AI diffusion.
Another question is open-source software – not just how many derivative models are built off DeepSeek or Llama models, but rather how much of these are actually used in productive processes. Part of the beauty of studying AI is that it’s always changing, so that’s what keeps me interested and pushes me to keep translating Chinese language articles on AI on a weekly basis.

data.title: How lower US interest rates can help Hongkongers amid steady local prime ratesMost new mortgages, many corporate loans are based on interbank rates, which will continue to fall, monetary authority’s d
data.description:
Hong Kong banks may not cut their prime rates this year, but local interbank rates will still follow US rates lower, benefiting a majority of borrowers, according to a senior executive of the Hong Kong Monetary Authority (HKMA).
Analysts widely expect the US Federal Reserve to cut its key rate by three-quarters of a point this year, but Hong Kong commercial banks are unlikely to cut their prime lending rates more than they already have.
“It would be hard for banks to cut their prime rate any further because they can no longer cut their savings rate, which is now close to zero,” said HKMA deputy chief executive Arthur Yuen Kowk-hang.
“If they cut the prime rate but not their savings rates, it would narrow their net interest margins, which would affect their interest income. From a risk-management perspective, it is not ideal for the banks to do so.”
Yuen said the current net interest margin of Hong Kong lenders was about 1.4 per cent, which was among the narrowest worldwide; in most major economies it is above 2 per cent.
The HKMA has followed the Fed’s monetary policy since 1983 under its linked exchange-rate system, but commercial banks retain discretion in setting their prime and deposit rates.
Local commercial banks trimmed their prime rate – the lending rate offered to their best customers – by 25 basis points in 2025 after a 62.5-basis-point cut in 2024. They also cut their savings rates accordingly.
After the latest rate cut in October, Bank of China (Hong Kong), HSBC and its subsidiary Hang Seng Bank set their prime rate at 5 per cent, while Bank of East Asia, Standard Chartered and ICBC (Asia) maintained their prime rate at 5.25 per cent. Savings rates remained at 0.001 per cent.
This left Hong Kong’s prime rate at its historical low of 5 per cent, which was seen from 2009 to 2018 and again from 2019 to November 2022.
However, Yuen said that a majority of mortgage loans and corporate loans were not priced on the prime rate, but linked with the Hong Kong interbank offered rate (Hibor).

Over 90 per cent of new mortgage loans in recent years, and most corporate loans, were linked with the one-month Hibor, Yuen said. Only a small number of mortgage loans, corporate loans for small and medium-sized enterprises, and personal loans were priced on the prime rate, he added.
“The Hibor rate usually goes down with US interest rates,” he said. “As such, the US rate cut this year would bring down the cost of funding for most mortgage borrowers and corporate borrowers. A lower interest rate would be positive for local economic activity.”
The one-month Hibor stood at 2.79 per cent on Friday, compared with 3.75 per cent a year earlier.
Yuen said a lower interest rate would be positive for loan demand, but that would not necessarily help reduce bad debts.
“It is too early to predict that the bad debt will peak, as many corporations still face a lot of difficulties and challenges,” he said. “The US interest rate is still full of uncertainties, and there are a lot of geopolitical tensions.”
The bad debt ratio of the Hong Kong banking sector was 1.98 per cent at the end of September, slightly up from 1.97 per cent at the end of June, which is still below the 2 per cent average for the past two decades.
The bad debt ratio for mainland-related lending decreased to 1.99 per cent in September from 2.16 per cent a quarter earlier. Yuen said this reflected that bad debts related to local lending increased during the July to September quarter.
Wilson Chan, adjunct professor at City University and a three-decade banker before joining academia, said the lower interest rate would help reduce bad debt.
“At a lower cost of funding, more companies would get bank loans to develop their business,” Chan said. “We have seen that the worst is over for the property sector, and property prices have stabilised. The retail and restaurant sector also benefited from the increasing number of tourists.”

data.title: China’s central bank to support doubling of yuan liquidity for Hong Kong lenders
data.description:
China’s central bank will support Hong Kong’s monetary authority to double the amount of yuan liquidity available to local banks, helping them increase yuan lending and enhancing the city’s role as an international offshore centre for the mainland currency.
People’s Bank of China (PBOC) deputy governor Zou Lan said at the Asian Financial Forum (AFF) in Hong Kong on Monday that the central bank would provide more funding to allow the Hong Kong Monetary Authority (HKMA) to double the yuan liquidity facility to 200 billion yuan (US$28.68 billion) from next week.
In addition, the country would issue more yuan sovereign bonds in Hong Kong to meet international investors’ demand for the currency, he added.
“Hong Kong has been the world’s largest and most influential offshore yuan trading centre,” Zou said in a speech at the opening of the AFF. “The PBOC considers Hong Kong important and is firmly committed to continuing to roll out different measures to increase yuan liquidity.”
Launched in October, the facility, officially known as the RMB Business Facility, provides banks with cheap and stable yuan funding so that they can offer yuan loans to global customers.
The expansion was much needed, as the HKMA had already allocated 100 billion yuan to 40 banks in December, it said in a statement. The doubling would allow existing banks to get more liquidity and support the addition of more banks to the scheme, it added.
The HKMA said the new quota would be effective from February 2.
“The increase of the facility size to 200 billion yuan enables the HKMA to provide timely and sufficient yuan liquidity to meet market-development needs and assist banks to enhance their yuan business,” said HKMA chief executive Eddie Yue Wai-man.

According to the HKMA, banks had reported that the facility not only served companies in Hong Kong but also successfully channelled offshore yuan funds to regions such as Southeast Asia, the Middle East and Europe.
Yue said the expansion of the facility “would reinforce Hong Kong’s status as an international financial centre and global offshore yuan business hub”.
PBOC’s Zou also said the central bank would support Hong Kong’s yuan clearing bank, Bank of China (Hong Kong), to issue negotiable certificates of deposit onshore, thereby expanding its access to liquidity across various maturities.
“This will strengthen the clearing bank’s service capabilities and contribute positively to offshore yuan liquidity conditions,” Yue said.
As of December, the yuan was the second most-used currency in global trade finance, with an 8.3 per cent share, second only to the US dollar at 79.53 per cent, according to Swift, the banking industry’s messaging network system. The yuan’s share was 6 per cent in December 2024 and only 2.1 per cent four years ago.
In terms of all types of payments, the yuan ranked sixth worldwide in December, with a share of 2.73 per cent, behind the US dollar (50.49 per cent), euro (21.9 per cent), British pound (6.73 per cent), Canadian dollar (3.44 per cent) and Japanese yen (3.42 per cent).
Hong Kong was the largest offshore yuan market worldwide as of December, with 74.55 per cent of offshore yuan payments, followed by the UK at 7.04 per cent and Singapore at 4.39 per cent, according to Swift.

data.title: Xiaomi’s SU7 becomes China’s first Tesla killer, outselling the Model 3
data.description:
After five years of playing catch-up, China has produced a worthy “Tesla killer”, a massive achievement for the country’s premium electric vehicle (EV) makers that is set to stoke national pride.
Sales of Xiaomi’s fully electric SU7 on the mainland stood at 258,164 last year, nearly 30 per cent higher than the Model 3’s deliveries of 200,361 units, according to data from the China Passenger Car Association (CPCA).
Since its debut in China at the end of 2019, the Model 3, assembled at the Gigafactory 3 in Shanghai’s Lingang free-trade zone, led the country’s premium electric sedan category until 2024, despite massive efforts to unseat it by rivals, including Nio, Xpeng and IM Motors.
“Tesla’s dominance in the premium EV segment has been eroded by its Chinese competitors that are able to churn out vehicles on par with its technology standards while offering them at lower prices,” said Eric Han, a senior manager at Shanghai consultancy Suolei. “Xiaomi’s success is a strong boost for Chinese carmakers, which are all trying to move up the value chain.”

Tesla’s Shanghai factory also assembles the Model Y, which outsold all other SUVs – including petrol-powered rivals – on the mainland in 2025.
Chinese EV builders, led by BYD, the world’s largest EV maker, saw increased deliveries as their lower-priced cars proved popular with millions of middle and low-income drivers.
Only a handful of the country’s 50-odd EV makers are profitable due to heavy spending on research and development, with a vicious price war squeezing profit margins further.
Xiaomi, a smartphone vendor and EV start-up, began delivering its SU7 vehicles in March 2024. The popularity of its phones helped spur sales of the intelligent car featuring a preliminary autonomous driving system and a sophisticated digital cockpit.
The basic edition of the Model 3 is priced at 235,500 yuan, 9 per cent higher than the SU7.
In 2025, Tesla’s deliveries on the mainland fell 4.8 per cent from a year earlier to 625,698 units. The sales represented 4.8 per cent of the total EV sales nationwide, according to the CPCA.
Tesla’s market share in China topped 16 per cent in 2020 when the Shanghai factory began producing the Model 3. That dropped to 6.9 per cent in 2024 amid mounting com­petition from home-grown firms, which developed dozens of ­premium models with intelligent features similar to those in Tesla’s cars.
“Because the Model Y remains the bestselling SUV in China, it is too early to conclude Tesla cannot regain its lost ground,” said Gao Shen, an independent analyst in Shanghai, adding that domestic carmakers were either under pressure to break even or faced doubts regarding their cars’ safety and reliability.
In March, three people died in an accident involving an SU7 in Tongling, Anhui province, while the vehicle’s driver assistance system was turned on.
The crash prompted mainland authorities to tighten their oversight of early-stage autonomous driving technologies.
In October, another accident involving an SU7 Ultra model killed a 31-year-old man who was suspected of drink driving in Chengdu, Sichuan province. According to local media reports, passers-by tried to pull him out of the burning car, but were unable to open the doors.
Xiaomi founder and CEO Lei Jun said in November that the company would prioritise safety while designing and building new models.
Earlier this month, Xiaomi unveiled a refreshed version of the SU7, with a range of more than 900km on a single charge, to sustain its sales momentum.
Presales of the refreshed SU7 were launched at 229,900 yuan – 14,000 yuan, or 6.5 per cent, higher than the existing version.
The top edition offers a driving range of 902km, compared with 830km for the existing Pro variant.

data.title: Cyclist fatally hit by car in Hong Kong, the second in a week
data.description:
A cyclist has died in hospital after being knocked down by a car in Tai Po, a week after another fatal accident involving a bicycle in Hong Kong.
Police received a report of the accident at 7.25am on Monday.
The victim, a man in his sixties, was struck by the car on Ting Kok Road near Tai Po Industrial Estate.
He was rushed to Alice Ho Miu Ling Nethersole Hospital in Tai Po for treatment but was later pronounced dead.
The force subsequently arrested the driver for dangerous driving.

Last week, a 55-year-old female cyclist died after being hit by a van in Sheung Shui.
Dashcam footage circulating online showed the cyclist riding across a pedestrian crossing against a red light when she was struck on her right side by the black van.

data.title: Jensen Huang celebrates Lunar New Year with Nvidia employees in Shanghai
data.description:
Jensen Huang marked the coming Lunar New Year with Nvidia employees in Shanghai on Saturday, as the CEO of the world’s most valuable company made his first trip to China in 2026 amid uncertainties around its H200 graphics processing unit (GPU).
Huang received a rock-star welcome when he joined Nvidia’s annual Lunar New Year celebration, according to two employees who asked not to be named as they were not authorised to speak to the media. Huang gave a speech and handed out tangerines that he bought at a market earlier in the day, while staff members rushed to get his autograph, one employee said.
Huang is “celebrating Chinese New Year with our local employees in China”, an Nvidia spokesperson told the Post on Sunday, without elaborating on his agenda.
The latest move came as Beijing is expected to allow imports of the company’s H200, its second most powerful GPU, after temporarily holding the chips at the border.
The GPU, which remains an ideal option for Chinese big tech companies such as Alibaba Group Holding and ByteDance to train their artificial intelligence models, got Washington’s green light for exports earlier this month, on condition that the shipments not exceed 50 per cent of the volume sold domestically to US customers. Alibaba owns the Post.
The 62-year-old Huang began his latest trip to China earlier this week, after finishing his visit to the World Economic Forum in Davos, Switzerland. He toured the chipmaker’s Shanghai office and met with staff to review Nvidia’s milestones and future product pipelines, Tencent Holdings’ news portal reported on Friday.
However, Huang did not mention the H200 to staff in Shanghai, according to the report.
Huang stopped at a grocery store in Shanghai’s busy Lujiazui area on Saturday, according to photos circulated on social media. He was seen shopping, tasting various fruits and looking relaxed.

Huang is next expected to visit Nvidia’s staff in Beijing and Shenzhen, continuing a routine he has followed over the past two years to mark the Lunar New Year with employees and business partners.
He was expected to arrive in Beijing on Monday, with the office’s annual Lunar New Year celebration taking place that evening, according to an employee.
Chinese authorities recently granted “in-principle” approval for tech giants including Alibaba, Tencent and ByteDance to purchase the H200 chips, Bloomberg reported on Friday. However, the firms were also encouraged to buy a certain amount of domestically produced chips, the report said.
With demand rising, some Chinese AI developers have turned to unofficial channels to secure the chips. One reseller said he had received “a couple of inquiries” earlier this week for the H200, adding that a server bundle containing eight H200 GPUs was selling for about 2.3 million yuan (US$330,403) – roughly 50 per cent above the official list price in mainland China, the Post previously reported.
